config.title: Deutsches Technikmuseum: FAQ
config.subtitle: Tickets, hours, the rooftop plane and how to plan the giant technology museum in Kreuzberg.

items.q: How much do Deutsches Technikmuseum tickets cost?
items.a: An adult ticket costs €15 online or €17 at the door, because the door price includes a €2 service fee. Reduced tickets are €9 online, everyone under 18 enters free with a booked 0-euro ticket, and the Museum Pass Berlin is accepted.

items.q: Is the Technikmuseum free for children?
items.a: Yes. All visitors under 18 enter free, but each child still needs a free 0-euro ticket, which you can book online together with the adult tickets.

items.q: When is the Deutsches Technikmuseum closed?
items.a: It is closed every Monday. Otherwise it runs Tuesday to Friday 9:00–17:30 and Saturday, Sunday and most holidays 10:00–18:00, with last admission at 17:00. On the first Friday of each month entry is free for everyone from 13:00 if you book the free ticket online.

items.q: Does the ticket include the Science Center Spectrum?
items.a: Yes. The same day ticket covers both the Technikmuseum and the Science Center Spectrum, the hands-on experiment building at Möckernstraße 26. With children in the group, Spectrum is usually the strongest part of the visit.

items.q: How long do you need for the Technikmuseum?
items.a: The official average visit is about 2.5 hours, but the complex can fill a whole day. The realistic plan is to pick three or four halls in advance, for example Aviation, the rail roundhouses and Shipping, and treat the rest as a reason to come back.

items.q: How do I get to the Deutsches Technikmuseum?
items.a: Take the U1, U3 or U7 to Möckernbrücke or the U1, U2 or U3 to Gleisdreieck, both a short walk away, or the S1, S2, S25 or S26 to Anhalter Bahnhof. The main entrance is Trebbiner Straße 9; the Ladestraße and Spectrum entrance is Möckernstraße 26. Skip driving: parking nearby is very limited and fees apply from 1 August 2026.

items.q: What is the plane on the roof of the Technikmuseum?
items.a: It is a Douglas C-47 Skytrain, the transport type Berliners call the Rosinenbomber or raisin bomber. It has stood on the roof of the New Building since May 1999 as a memorial to the 1948–49 Berlin Airlift, and the aviation exhibition ends on the roof terrace directly beneath it.

items.q: Is the Technikmuseum worth it with kids?
items.a: Yes, it is one of the best family museums in Berlin: under-18s are free, the aviation and rail halls are full of real machines, and the included Science Center Spectrum is entirely hands-on. It is also a strong rainy-day choice because almost everything is indoors.
